title: DNS: The Internet's Phone Book
intro: Your friend says 'My website is down! Nobody can reach it!' It's like knowing a restaurant's name but not its address -- without DNS, your browser can't find anything. You'll investigate and discover how DNS -- the system that translates domain names into IP addresses -- actually works.

teach.title: DNS Is Like a Phone Book
teach.body: Imagine you want to call a pizza place. You know its name ('Mario's Pizza') but not its phone number. So you look it up in a phone book. **DNS** (Domain Name System) works exactly like this. You know the name (`google.com`) and DNS looks up the number (`142.250.80.46`) so your browser can connect.

teach.title: DNS Is Like a GPS for the Internet
teach.body: When you type an address into your car's GPS, it doesn't just know where '123 Elm Street' is -- it looks it up in a huge map database and gives you directions. **DNS** does the same thing for web addresses. You type 'youtube.com' and DNS looks it up in its massive directory, then points your browser to the right server. Without DNS, you'd need to memorize the coordinates of every place you want to visit.

teach.title: Why Do We Need DNS?
teach.body: Computers find each other using **IP addresses** -- numbers like `192.168.1.1`. But humans are terrible at remembering numbers. DNS lets us use friendly names like `youtube.com` instead of `142.250.190.78`. Every time you visit a website, a DNS lookup happens first -- your browser asks 'What's the IP address for this name?'

teach.title: The DNS Lookup Process
teach.body:
When you type `example.com`, here's what happens:

1. Your browser checks its **cache** -- did I look this up recently?
2. If not, it asks your **router** (your home Wi-Fi)
3. If the router doesn't know, it asks your **ISP's DNS server**
4. If nobody knows, it goes all the way to the **root DNS servers** that know everything
5. The answer travels back down the chain and gets cached for next time

When you launch an app and buy a domain, DNS is what connects your brand name to your actual server. Understanding DNS means you can vibecode, deploy, and go live without panicking when your domain doesn't work immediately.
DNS translates human-readable domain names (like google.com) into machine-readable IP addresses (like 142.250.80.46). Without DNS, you'd have to memorize numbers for every website.

start.narrative: Your friend just launched their website at www.coolstore.com. They say: 'I bought the domain an hour ago and pointed it to my server, but when I type the URL I get an error: DNS_PROBE_FINISHED_NXDOMAIN.' What does this error mean?

choices.text: DNS couldn't find an IP address for that domain name
The server is down and not responding
The website code has a bug

wrong-server-down.narrative: Not quite. If the server were down, you'd get a different error -- like a timeout or connection refused. The NXDOMAIN error happens earlier in the process: your browser can't even find WHERE the server is. It's a DNS problem, not a server problem.

choices.text: Got it -- DNS couldn't resolve the domain to an IP

wrong-code-bug.narrative: A code bug would only matter if your browser could actually reach the server. The NXDOMAIN error happens before any connection is made -- your browser is still trying to figure out WHERE to send the request. It's stuck at the DNS step.

choices.text: OK, so the domain name itself isn't resolving — it's a DNS problem

verify-records.narrative: Before assuming propagation, you want to verify the records were set correctly. How do you check the actual DNS configuration?

choices.text: Run dig coolstore.com @8.8.8.8 to query Google's resolver and see exactly what record (or lack of record) is returned
Use an online DNS checker like dnschecker.org to see how the record looks from many regions
Just refresh the browser a few times and hope it resolves

check-propagation.narrative: You confirm the A record is set correctly at the registrar — it points to the right IP. But your local resolver still returns NXDOMAIN. What's most likely going on?

choices.text: DNS propagation: the new record exists at the authoritative servers but caching resolvers haven't picked it up yet — can take up to 48 hours
Your local OS is caching the old NXDOMAIN — flush it with sudo dscacheutil -flushcache (mac) or ipconfig /flushdns (windows)
The registrar must be lying — open a support ticket immediately

ttl-step.narrative: Your friend wants to switch hosting providers next month and is worried about another long propagation delay. The current TTL on the A record is 86400 seconds (24 hours). What should they do before the migration?

choices.text: Lower the TTL to 300 seconds (5 minutes) a day or two before the cutover, then raise it back after the new IP propagates
Leave the TTL alone — it's only 24 hours, that's tolerable
Set TTL to 0 permanently so updates are instant

monitor-step.narrative: The site is finally live and resolving worldwide. Your friend asks: 'How will I know if DNS breaks again in the future?' What do you recommend?

choices.text: Set up an uptime monitor that hits the domain from multiple regions every minute and alerts on resolution failures
Bookmark dnschecker.org and check it once a week
Wait until users complain — DNS rarely breaks on its own

excellent-end.narrative: You walked the full DNS lifecycle: identified NXDOMAIN as a resolution failure, verified the record with dig, explained propagation, lowered TTL ahead of the next migration, and set up multi-region monitoring. Your friend now understands DNS as a system rather than a black box.
excellent-end.feedback: This is how senior engineers debug DNS: confirm the record exists at the authority, distinguish caching from misconfiguration, plan TTL changes around migrations, and monitor from outside your network. NXDOMAIN is a starting point, not a verdict.

mediocre-end.narrative: The site works and you understand the basics, but a manual weekly check won't catch a 3am outage on a Sunday. The first regional DNS issue takes 8 hours to discover.
mediocre-end.feedback: Manual checks are better than nothing, but DNS issues often hit specific regions or resolvers. A continuous monitor that pings from multiple geographies is what catches the bugs you can't see from your own desk.

weak-end.narrative: Days later, the site silently disappears for users on a specific ISP because of a misconfigured caching resolver. Your friend finds out from an angry customer.
weak-end.feedback: DNS does break on its own — TTL expirations, registrar errors, resolver outages. Treat DNS like any other production dependency: monitor it, alert on failures, and rehearse the change-window playbook.
